data.title: China's JD.com Targets $2.6 Billion German Takeover--And Investors Are Paying Attention
data.description:
JD.com is deep in talks to acquire German electronics chain Ceconomy AG (MTAGF) in a potential 2.2 billion ($2.6 billion) dealsignaling a calculated push beyond China's borders. The proposed offer stands at 4.60 per share in cash, a 23% premium to Ceconomy's last closing price of 3.75. While the offer isn't binding yet, it marks a renewed attempt by JD.com to build a stronger presence in Europe, after walking away from a bid for UK retailer Currys last year. Shares of Ceconomy surged as much as 15.5% on the news.

The road ahead, though, isn't guaranteed. JD.com has been sounding out Ceconomy's biggest shareholdersincluding legacy families like the Kellerhals and Haniel groupsbut some have shown reluctance to lock in firm commitments. Without their support, a formal bid could still stall. The revival of talks, first reported back in February, shows JD's sustained interest in the Frankfurt-listed company, particularly as it looks to counter slowing growth at home through international retail assets with operating scale.

Ceconomy runs over 1,000 stores across Europe under the MediaMarkt and Saturn brandsgiving JD.com a ready-made retail footprint if the deal closes. Analysts note the tie-up could unlock synergy between JD's logistics infrastructure and Ceconomy's physical presence on the ground. That combination may offer strategic value, especially in last-mile delivery and customer engagement. For now, investors will be watching whether JD.com can secure the shareholder backing it needs to take the next step.

data.title: India's Goyal hopeful of concluding US trade deal after 'fantastic' progress
data.description:
India is making "fantastic" progress in trade talks with the United States, Commerce Minister Piyush Goyal told Reuters on Thursday, adding he was very confident that India would get special treatment from President Donald Trump.

Trump threatened a 26% tariff on Indian imports in April but paused implementation to allow for talks. That pause ends on August 1, though India has yet to receive a formal tariff letter, unlike more than 20 other countries.

Earlier this week two Indian government sources said the prospects of an interim trade deal before the deadline had dimmed, as talks remain deadlocked over tariff cuts on key agricultural and dairy products.

In an interview during a visit to London, Goyal played down the importance of deadlines in trade talks but struck a bullish tone on how negotiations with the United States were going.

"We're making fantastic progress with the United States, and I do hope we'll be able to conclude a very consequential partnership," Goyal said.

Asked about possible disagreement with Washington over agriculture, Goyal said that "negotiations happen in the negotiation room", but said India wouldn't necessarily hold out for a comprehensive deal if smaller sectoral wins were possible.

"All options are on the table, and we will see how it plays out, but what is important is to get preferential market access over our competitors, our peers," he said.

"And I'm very confident that India, having been the first country to start negotiating, will be given a special and preferred treatment."

On Wednesday Trump announced a deal with Japan which lowers auto tariffs, though medicines and semiconductors will be negotiated separately.

Goyal was in London after signing a free trade deal with Britain, which has also negotiated sectoral tariff relief with the White House.

But while other countries may have secured deals with Trump ahead of India, despite Prime Minister Narendra Modi's efforts in February to kickstart trade talks with a White House visit, Goyal played down the significance of deadlines.

"I don't think any of the negotiations ever worked with deadlines or the preconceived limitations," Goyal said, adding he wanted a win-win deal.

"One has to have a broad mind and a package which is balanced and can give equity to both sides."

data.title: SouthState Boosts Dividend by 11%
data.description:
By Kelly Cloonan

SouthState has raised its quarterly dividend to 60 cents a share from 54 cents.

The new payout, equal to $2.40 a year, represents an annual yield of about 2.5% based on Wednesday's closing price of $97.22.

The financial services company said Thursday that the higher dividend will be paid on Aug. 15 to shareholders of record as of Aug. 8.

The company last increased its quarterly dividend about a year ago.

Write to Kelly Cloonan at kelly.cloonan@wsj.com

data.title: Oregon adoption policy on LGBTQ acceptance violates free speech rights, court says
data.description:
Key points:
  • Court rules Oregon policy violates free speech and religious rights
  • Would-be adoptive parent wins injunction against policy in her case

An Oregon woman seeking to adopt two children out of foster care will be allowed to move forward after a U.S. appeals court on Thursday said a state policy requiring adoptive parents to “respect, accept and support” the children’s gender expression and sexual orientation was unconstitutional.

A divided three-judge panel of the 9th U.S. Circuit Court of Appeals reversed a lower court ruling blocking Jessica Bates from adopting because of the Oregon Department of Human Services' policy.

The majority agreed with Bates, a devout Christian who said her beliefs prevent her from following the policy, finding that it violates Bates’ rights to free speech and free exercise of religion under the First Amendment of the U.S. Constitution.

Circuit Judge Daniel Bress, who was appointed by Republican President Donald Trump in his first term, joined with Circuit Judge Michael Hawkins, an appointee of Democratic President Bill Clinton, in finding that the policy is unconstitutional.

Oregon hasn’t explained why other ways to protect LGBTQ children in foster care — instead of a policy that broadly bans Bates from adopting — aren’t feasible, Bress wrote in the opinion.

“A state’s general conception of the child’s best interest does not create a force field against the valid operation of other constitutional rights,” he wrote.

Bates was represented by lawyers from Alliance Defending Freedom, a conservative Christian group that is routinely involved in high-profile court cases involving religious liberties.

“The 9th Circuit was right to remind Oregon that the foster and adoption system is supposed to serve the best interests of children, not the state’s ideological crusade,” ADF Senior Counsel and Vice President of Litigation Strategy Jonathan Scruggs said in a statement after the ruling.

A spokesperson for Democratic Oregon Attorney General Dan Rayfield, whose office defended the policy, said in a statement they are reviewing the ruling to determine their next steps.

Bates filed the lawsuit in 2023 after her application to adopt a pair of siblings was denied when she told an administrator that she couldn’t support the behavior of a hypothetical child whose preferred pronouns and identity don’t match their biological sex.

The district court rejected Bates’ bid for an injunction against the policy, finding that Oregon has a strong interest in protecting the rights of LGBTQ children and the policy’s regulation of free speech was precise enough.

Circuit Judge Richard Clifton, an appointee of former President George W. Bush, dissented, saying that the policy regulates parental actions and does not impact speech beyond what is necessary.

The case is Bates v. Pakseresht, case number 23-4169 in the 9th U.S. Circuit Court of Appeals.

For Bates: Jonathan Scruggs, James Campbell, Johannes Widmalm-Delphonse, John Bursch of the Alliance Defending Freedom and Rebekah Schultheiss of the Freedom Foundation

For Oregon: Philip Thoennes, Denise Fjordbeck, Benjamin Gutman, Ellen Rosenblum of the Oregon Department of Justice

data.title: Latam currencies muted, stocks mixed as US trade developments cloud outlook
data.description:
Key points:
  • Chile's cenbank to cut rates by 25 bps next week, traders say
  • Focus on Mexico's Becle, Cemex earnings
  • Mexico inflation slows in early July, reigniting rate cut expectations
  • Turkish cenbank returns to easing with big 300-point rate cut

Most Latin American currencies were flat to lower on Thursday, while stocks were mixed as investors monitored whether U.S. President Donald Trump might exempt the region from his proposed sweeping tariffs.

The region had faced some of the least impact from U.S. tariffs when Trump announced reciprocal measures on global economies this year. However, earlier this month, he threatened higher tariffs on both Mexico and Brazil, unnerving investors.

Mexican President Claudia Sheinbaum said on Thursday she hopes to reach an agreement to avoid U.S. tariffs before they take effect next week. Trump has threatened a 30% tariff on the country, excluding goods covered under a free-trade agreement.

Similarly, Brazilian President Luiz Inacio Lula da Silva said that his country is ready to negotiate with the United States, which has proposed a 50% tariff on Brazilian exports.

Both the Brazilian real and Mexico's peso were little changed in afternoon trading against a marginally higher dollar.

"There's not yet been any progress on the agreements with Mexico and Brazil, two of the largest economies of Latin America ... even though there's been progress about Asian trade, there is uncertainty about bilateral trade with the U.S. in the region," said Rachel Ziemba, founder of Ziemba Insights.

The currency of copper exporter Chile dipped 0.2% as a rally in prices of the red metal cooled.

Investors also evaluated a poll showing Chile's central bank could lower its benchmark interest rate by 25 basis points to 4.75% at its next meeting later this month.

Colombia's peso depreciated 0.9%, while Argentina's peso declined 1%.

On the equities front, Mexican equities gained 0.9% after data showed headline inflation rate eased in the first half of July, fuelling expectations of further interest rate cuts by the central bank.

"This could ease the central bank's dilemma somewhat. It reflects Mexico's focus on growth amid ongoing uncertainty, potentially giving the central bank more room to maneuver," Ziemba added.

Corporate earnings were also in focus in Mexico.

Cement producer Cemex added 3% after posting a double-digit increase in second-quarter profit, while Becle , the world's largest tequila producer, said it was seeing early signs of recovery in its main markets, sending its shares up 6.7%.

Meanwhile, Brazilian equities slid 1.2%, weighed down by financials, while Argentina's Merval index gained 1.1%.

Turkey's lira (TRYTOM=D4) was little changed, and domestic stocks jumped 1% after the local central bank cut interest rates by a larger-than-expected 300 basis points to 43%.

data.title: Treasury Yields Rise as Data, Trade Deals Foster Risk Taking — Market Talk
data.description:
1538 ET - U.S. labor data supports a long Fed hold and intensifies a Treasury selloff, pushing yields to a second consecutive rising session. Recent trade deals have been fueling risk-taking, too.Data show layoffs remain contained, removing a potential reason for the Fed to cut interest rates. President Trump, however, keeps calling for fast monetary easing, making markets suspect a more dovish stance lies ahead. June new-home sales grow less than expected, reversing May's decline. Durable goods orders, due tomorrow, are expected to contract 11.1% in a WSJ survey, following May's 16.4% expansion. The 10-year rises 0.021 percentage point to 4.408% and the two-year adds 0.041 p.p. to 3.924%. (paulo.trevisani@wsj.com; @ptrevisani)

1510 ET - Intel may have more room to cut capital and operating expenses, according to Bank of America's Vivek Arya. Intel said in April that it would reduce its gross capital spending target by $2 billion this year to $18 billion. "However, we see potential for CEO Tan to further revise opex/capex down by 10-15%, opex toward $16bn vs. current $17bn, and capex toward $14-15bn vs. $18bn," Arya writes ahead of the company's earnings report. "While these measures could bring semblance of profit in the near-term," Arya notes concerns including "constant roadmap changes" and employee churn. Arya expects Intel's 2Q results to benefit from tariff pull-ins and a more favorable mix, but the chip company may face a tougher 2H as benefits reverse. (josh.beckerman@wsj.com)

1457 ET - The CD Howe Institute's monetary-policy committee says the Bank of Canada should leave its policy interest rate unchanged next week at 2.75% but follow that up with a cut in September. The Toronto-based committee is made up of economists from academia and the private sector, and their recommendation represents the median vote of economists in attendance. U.S. tariffs and U.S.-Canada negotiations on a tariff resolution dominated the discussions, the CD Howe think says in a summary of deliberations. Members expressed surprise about underlying economic resiliency, although adding that cracks are starting to surface. Still, a surprisingly strong jobs report for July and elevated core CPI were the key factors persuading committee members to recommend a BOC hold. (paul.vieira@wsj.com; @paulvieira)

1433 ET - O'Reilly Automotive customers are willing to pay higher prices for essential car repairs because it's cheaper than buying a new vehicle, executives say. "The strong value proposition of maintaining and repairing an existing vehicle, coupled with the high quality of vehicles, creates a very compelling incentive for our customers to prioritize their auto part spend," CEO Brad Beckham says during an analyst call. O'Reilly raised prices to mitigate new tariff costs, and increasingly complex car repairs are sending up prices, Beckham says. While there's some pullback on discretionary purchases, consumers are willing to shell out for a new alternator or battery.(katherine.hamilton@wsj.com)

1430 ET - O'Reilly Automotive is clocking some uncertainty among consumers despite a broader market recovery from a volatile start to this year. The car part provider says its discretionary category, which makes up a relatively small portion of its business, showed softness in 2Q. "The continued sluggishness in these categories is an indicator that consumers are still remaining cautious and conservative in how they are managing the spend in the current environment," CEO Brad Beckham says on an analyst call. He says O'Reilly's guidance is conservative to account for the possibility that consumers will pull back more in the second half of the year if prices rise. (katherine.hamilton@wsj.com)

1419 ET - O'Reilly Automotive is worried about inflation. Management is keeping a close eye on how tariffs raise prices across other industries because it is concerned that will put pressure on how much consumers are willing to spend on non-essential repairs like fixing their air conditioning or getting an oil change, CFO Jeremy Fletcher says. O'Reilly is logging pressure in its discretionary category, despite strength elsewhere in the business. Executives say they are still unsure if inflationary pressure has gotten worse in recent months. Low-income consumers are particularly squeezed. "Our goal is to minimize any impact to our consumer because we know they're under pressure," President Brent Kirby says. O'Reilly is up 4% after raising its sales outlook for the year as it logged higher 2Q revenue.(katherine.hamilton@wsj.com)

1354 ET - Another index gauging Canadian consumer sentiment is on the upswing for July. The Conference Board of Canada's consumer-confidence index rose in July to 62.7. This marks a fourth-straight month-over-month increase after hitting its lowest level on record in March, at 44.2. The board notes that despite the increases, the index reading remains below year-ago levels. Still, the reading suggests some resiliency among Canadian households. The index' climb comes on the same day that Statistics Canada reports that it estimates retail sales likely jumped 1.6% in June, following a 1.1% drop in May. The board's confidence-index summary says the increase is tied to more favorable macroeconomic signals, such as easing price pressures and labor-market gains. (Paul.Vieira@wsj.com, @paulvieira)

1350 ET - The second largest chicken processor in the U.S., Pilgrim's Pride, is spending $400 million to build a new facility in Georgia. Pilgrim's, which slaughters roughly a fifth of U.S. chicken, says the plant will employ 630 people, producing a variety of fully cooked chicken products to support the growth of its prepared foods business. The project is expected to finish in 2027. Pilgrim's has been ramping up its line of cooked, branded chicken products in recent years, including its Just Bare chicken line and Pilgrim's-branded chicken nuggets. The company is majority owned by JBS, the world's largest meatpacker. JBS said in May it was building a cooked sausage plant for its prepared foods business in Iowa. (patrick.thomas@wsj.com)

1329 ET - Lockheed Martin appears to have inadequate control and oversight of its big-budget programs, which it had previously assured investors were substantially derisked, Gimme Credit's Carol Levenson says in a note. Those signals are even more disturbing than the size of its 2Q write-downs on those programs, Levenson says. Management says the losses were driven by its new CFO coming in and giving the programs additional scrutiny, but Levenson finds that explanation unsatisfying. "Charges of this magnitude should not arise because of one personnel change," she says. Lockheed also revealed that the IRS in May sought $4.6 billion in additional income taxes related to accounting changes in 2018--that should have been disclosed when the notice was received, not two months later, Levenson says. (dean.seal@wsj.com)

1306 ET - American Airlines and Southwest Airlines, carriers with the heaviest exposure to domestic travel, are taking the brunt of a selloff in U.S. airline stocks. Southwest, the nation's largest carrier of domestic passengers, reported lower 2Q revenue and earnings after sluggish domestic flying earlier in the year stabilized but failed to rebound. American reports slightly higher 2Q revenue but says weak domestic leisure travel offset higher premium and international demand. American gets about 70% of its revenue from domestic flying, which is outsized exposure compared with its network peers, Melius Research analysts Conor Cunningham and Patrick Coleman say in a research note. Southwest slides 13% to while American drops 10%. Delta Air Lines is off 2.3% while United Airlines is flat. Smaller carriers are seeing 3% to 6% stock declines. (dean.seal@wsj.com)

1253 ET - Barbie is making her way back to the silver screen, this time in animation. Mattel is partnering with Illumination--the studio behind the "Despicable Me" franchise and "The Super Mario Bros. Movie"--on the new film, which isn't yet dated. In addition to its partnership with Illumination, Mattel recently tapped "Wicked"-filmmaker Jon M. Chu to direct a live-action Hot Wheels movie, and began collaborating with OpenAI to develop AI-powered toys and experiences around its portfolio of brands. "We are partnering with best-in-class, global leaders in their domain," Mattel CEO Ynon Kreiz says. These partnerships, he says, will "reimagine how far our brands can go." Mattel shares fall 13% after dialing back its full-year outlook. (connor.hart@wsj.com)

1252 ET - Chipotle says macro pressure impacted the lower-income consumer in 2Q, but BTIG points out the company's restaurant margin was modestly better at 27.4%. Long term, the company expects to return to mid-single-digit comp growth, but a macro tailwind might be necessary to get to that level, analysts Peter Saleh and Ben Parente say. They are "cautiously optimistic" that increases in loyalty members, catering efforts and limited-time offers will drive a meaningful change in comparable sales entering next year. Chipotle is off 14%. (natalie.weger@wsj.com; @NatalieWeger)

data.title: RPM INTERNATIONAL INC/DE/ SEC 10-K Report
data.description:
RPM International Inc., a global leader in specialty coatings, sealants, building materials, and related services, has released its annual Form 10-K report for the fiscal year ended May 31, 2025. The report provides a comprehensive overview of the company's financial performance, business operations, strategic initiatives, and the challenges it faces in the competitive market.

Financial Highlights

  • Net Sales: $7,372.6 million, reflecting a 0.5% increase from the previous year, driven by growth in the CPG and PCG segments despite declines in the Consumer and SPG segments.
  • Gross Profit: $3,050.5 million, with a gross profit margin of 41.4%, up from 41.1% in the prior year, primarily due to savings from MAP 2025 initiatives.
  • Operating Income: $865.2 million, reflecting improvements from MAP 2025 initiatives, partially offset by increased SG&A expenses and unfavorable foreign currency translation.
  • Net Income: $690.3 million, representing a 17.1% increase from the previous year, supported by a lower effective income tax rate of 12.9% compared to 25.2% in the prior year.
  • Diluted EPS: $5.35, up from $4.56 in the previous year, reflecting the overall improvement in net income and operational efficiencies.

Business Highlights

  • Revenue Segments: RPM operates through four reportable segments: Construction Products Group (CPG), Performance Coatings Group (PCG), Consumer Group, and Specialty Products Group (SPG). CPG accounted for approximately 38% of consolidated net sales, PCG for 20%, Consumer for 33%, and SPG for 9%.
  • Geographical Performance: Approximately 30% of RPM's sales are generated in international markets, with products marketed in 163 countries and territories. The company operates manufacturing facilities in 118 locations across various countries, including Argentina, Australia, Belgium, Brazil, Canada, and more.
  • Sales Units: The CPG segment generated $2.8 billion in net sales, PCG $1.5 billion, Consumer $2.4 billion, and SPG $0.7 billion for the fiscal year ended May 31, 2025.
  • New Product Launches: The company introduced new residential home weatherization systems under the TUFF-N-DRI, Watchdog Waterproofing, and Enviro-Dri brand names, and expanded its product offerings in the food coatings business.
  • New Production Launches: RPM invested in plant consolidations and shared production, distribution, and R&D centers as part of its MAP 2025 program, enhancing operational efficiencies and international coordination.
  • Future Outlook: RPM anticipates continued inflationary headwinds impacting raw materials and labor costs in fiscal 2026. The company plans to implement alternative sourcing strategies to mitigate these impacts and expects to incur additional charges related to the completion of MAP 2025 projects.

Strategic Initiatives

  • MAP 2025 Initiative: The company implemented the MAP 2025 initiative, a multi-year restructuring plan aimed at improving margins by streamlining business processes, reducing working capital, and enhancing salesforce effectiveness. This initiative also included plant consolidations and investments in shared production, distribution, and R&D centers to improve operational efficiencies.
  • Capital Management: During fiscal 2025, RPM engaged in significant capital management activities, including the acquisition of the Star Brands Group, which contributed to a $580.2 million increase in cash used for business acquisitions. The company also repurchased $70.3 million of its own shares under the stock repurchase program and increased its cash dividends to $1.99 per share, reflecting a commitment to returning value to shareholders. Furthermore, the company borrowed $418.1 million on revolving credit facilities and $60.0 million on its accounts receivable securitization program to finance acquisitions.
  • Future Outlook: Looking ahead, RPM plans to continue its strategic focus on operational efficiency and margin improvement through the completion of MAP 2025 projects in fiscal 2026. The company also intends to maintain its capital investment strategy, with a focus on growth initiatives and enhancing production capabilities. Additionally, RPM will continue to explore opportunities for acquisitions that align with its strategic objectives, as evidenced by the recent acquisition of Ready Seal Inc.

Challenges and Risks

  • Economic and Strategic Risks: RPM is vulnerable to global and regional economic conditions, including market declines, public health crises, civil unrest, inflation, interest rates, and natural disasters. These factors could adversely affect operations, financial condition, and liquidity. The shift to online shopping and remote work may impact construction markets, potentially decreasing product demand.
  • Financial Risks: The company faces risks related to accounting estimates, pension costs, and significant indebtedness, which could impact cash flow and financial flexibility. Volatility in equity markets or interest rates could increase pension costs and required contributions.
  • Operational Risks: RPM's operating improvement initiatives, such as the Margin Achievement Plan 2025, may increase operational risks and expenses. Fluctuations in raw material supply and costs, as well as competitive pressures, could negatively impact financial results.
  • Regulatory Risks: Compliance with environmental, health, and safety laws, as well as evolving data protection laws, poses significant risks. Failure to comply could result in substantial costs, reputational damage, and legal liabilities.
  • Emerging Risks: Cybersecurity threats and the use of artificial intelligence present new challenges. RPM must manage data privacy compliance and protect its intellectual property from unauthorized access.
  • Management Strategies: Management acknowledges the challenges posed by economic uncertainties, competitive pressures, and regulatory compliance. The company is focused on strategic initiatives to enhance operational efficiency and manage risks related to raw material costs and supply chain disruptions. RPM is also addressing the potential impact of climate change and sustainability goals, recognizing the importance of adapting to regulatory changes and stakeholder expectations. Measures to mitigate risks include diversifying raw material sources, enhancing cybersecurity measures, and pursuing strategic acquisitions to strengthen its market position.

data.title: Elon Musk’s Grok AI integrates with Kalshi, Paradigm-backed prediction market
data.description:
xAI, the artificial‑intelligence startup founded by Elon Musk, will integrate its Grok conversational model into Kalshi, the only CFTC‑regulated exchange for event‑based contracts, bringing real‑time AI analysis to retail traders wagering on everything from Fed decisions to Oscar winners.

The tie‑up is effective immediately, the companies said on X. Kalshi users will be able to query Grok’s large‑language model for onchain data, historical odds, and breaking headlines before placing trades, while xAI gains a live environment to stress‑test Grok’s reasoning and probability outputs.

Kalshi operates a federally regulated prediction market where traders buy shares of “yes” or “no” contracts on future events, with stakes settling at $1 per share if correct and zero if wrong. The venue accepts crypto deposits in bitcoin, SOL, USDC, and WLD, but settles all wagers in U.S. dollars. Founded in 2018 by MIT alumni Tarek Mansour and Luana Lopes Lara, the New York‑based firm processed $1.4 billion in trades over 11 days for election-related contracts, a November 2024 report from the FIA Expo stated.

xAI builds Grok, an LLM positioned as a challenger to OpenAI’s ChatGPT and embedded inside Musk‑owned social platform X.

The partnership follows Kalshi’s $185 million Series B round in June 2025, led by crypto VC firm Paradigm, that valued the platform at $2 billion. Musk’s AI studio was valued on paper at $80 billion after an all-stock acquisition of X, Axios reported. “Two of the fastest-growing companies in America are now on the same team,” the official xAI account posted on X.

Kalshi’s collaboration with xAI occurs amid ongoing debates in Washington about expanding retail access to event contracts. The CFTC reopened public comments on political-election markets in June 2025 after a contentious debate sparked by Kalshi’s legal victory in 2024, which allowed it to offer election-related contracts after a federal appeals court ruling overturned a CFTC ban.

Meanwhile, prediction market rival Polymarket plans to re-enter the U.S. by acquiring derivatives exchange QCEX.

data.title: Subdued dollar firms after ECB leaves rates alone; tariffs and Fed in focus
data.description:
Key points:
  • Little reaction in euro after ECB holds rates steady, as expected
  • Expectations for higher rates support yen, but politics weigh
  • Expected trade deal with Europe in line with expectations

The dollar traded sideways versus the euro on Thursday after the European Central Bank held rates steady, and was confined to a tight range against the yen as prospects for higher Japanese rates offset worries about political risk after Sunday's elections.

The greenback showed fractional gains late in a subdued U.S. session, with investors girding for a busy news flow next week.

The European Central Bank left its policy rate at 2%, as expected, on Thursday, taking a break after a year of policy easing to wait for clarity over Europe's future trade relations with the United States.

"The view that the ECB is probably on hold here is probably gaining a bit more traction. We've trimmed expectations for the cuts in September to certainly less than 50/50," said Shaun Osborne, chief foreign exchange strategist at Scotiabank in Toronto.

The Japanese central bank's deputy governor, Shinichi Uchida, said Tuesday's trade deal with Washington had reduced economic uncertainty, comments that fuelled optimism in the market about the potential resumption of interest rate hikes.

Analysts believe the yen will face persistent headwinds after Sunday's upper house election, with the opposition considering a no-confidence motion.

The European Union is nearing a deal that would impose a broad 15% tariff on EU goods, diplomats said. The rate, which could also extend to cars, would mirror the framework agreement the United States struck with Japan.

"The ECB faces a challenge that is quantitatively different from the BoJ's," said Thierry Wizman, global forex and rates strategist at Macquarie Group.

"The euro has appreciated by far more than the JPY so far in 2025, meaning that the disinflationary impulse from U.S. import tariffs may be greater in the EU than in Japan, or the ECB may suspect as much," he added.

PMI data showed fragility in France following budget-cut proposals there, but also resilience in Germany and other parts of the euro zone.

Data showed that German business activity continued to grow marginally in July.

"As of now, there has been very little tariff impact on the hard data," said Mohit Kumar, economist at Jefferies.

ECONOMIC FALLOUT

Meanwhile, risk assets rallied as the trade deals eased fears over the economic fallout of a global trade war.

Next week the Federal Open Market Committee meets and is expected to leave rates where they are as policy makers wait for the expected impact from tariffs on inflation and growth to show up. Traders are now pricing in a 60% chance of a quarter point September rate cut, according to CME's FedWatch tool.

A number of U.S. employment releases next week culminate with Friday's big June payrolls report, while the July Personal Consumption Expenditures Price Index and the first revision to 2nd quarter Gross Domestic Product could also move markets.

"A lot of event risk next week and not just from the Fed, we've got a lot of data next week as well, so that's probably going to shape expectations to some extent for September," Osborne said.

The euro was last off 0.03% $1.1766, near the $1.1830 high from earlier this month, which marked its strongest level in more than three years.

Against the yen , the dollar was 0.27% firmer at 146.88, having hit a two-week low earlier in the session at 145.86.

Olivier Korber, forex strategist at Societe Generale, expects the yen to strengthen further, citing support from the trade deal and prospects for higher interest rates.

Japanese Prime Minister Shigeru Ishiba denied on Wednesday he had decided to quit after a source and media reports said he planned to announce his resignation to take responsibility for a bruising upper house election defeat.

Currencies mostly shrugged off news that U.S. President Donald Trump, a vocal critic of Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell, will visit the central bank on Thursday, a surprise move that escalates tensions between the administration and the Fed.

The dollar index , which measures the greenback against a basket of six currencies including the euro and yen, rose 0.17% to 97.36.

In cryptocurrencies, bitcoin rose 1.17% to $119,376.30. Ethereum rose 4.62% to $3,735.62.

data.title: Altiplano Makes Application for Management Cease Trade Order As Part of the 2025 Audit Filing Process
data.description:
Edmonton, Alberta--(Newsfile Corp. - July 24, 2025) - Altiplano Metals Inc. (WKN: A2JNFG) ("Altiplano" or the "Company") today announces that it anticipates a delay in the filing of the financial statements required pursuant to Part 4 of National Instrument 51-102 Continuous Disclosure Obligations (the "Instrument"). The potential for delay arises from the Company's and its auditors' joint assessment of the time required to complete the remaining audit procedures and the auditors' review process, as well as the delayed receipt of financial information from Altiplano's Chilean subsidiaries. The Company currently anticipates that the audit will be completed by August 1, 2025. However, as the outcome of the auditor's partner-level review remains uncertain, there may be additional follow-up items that could impact the timeline. Accordingly, the Company has allowed for a potential completion date of up to August 30, 2025. As a precautionary measure, the Company is pursuing a Management Cease Trade Order ("MCTO") to provide additional time, if required. Both the Company and its auditors remain fully committed and are working diligently toward the completion and filing of the financial statements.

The Instrument requires that the Company's audited financial statements, MD&A, and Chief Executive Officer ("CEO") and Chief Financial Officer ("CFO") certifications relating to the audited financial statements for the year ended March 31, 2025 be filed by July 29, 2025. The Company and its auditor are in the process of completing the associated audit work to complete the audited consolidated financial statements. The Company expects that the audited financial statements, MD&A, and CEO and CFO certifications relating to the audited financial statements for the year ended March 31, 2025 (collectively, the "Required Filings") will be completed and filed on or before August 30, 2025.

In connection with the anticipated delays in making the Required Filings within the time periods mandated by the Instrument, the Company has made an application under National Policy 12-203 Management Cease Trade Orders ("NP 12-203") to the Alberta Securities Commission, as principal regulator for the Company, requesting that a MCTO be issued. If granted, the MCTO will restrict all trading by the Company's CEO and CFO in securities of the Company, whether direct or indirect. The issuance of the MCTO will not affect the ability of persons who are not directors, officers or insiders of the Company to trade their securities. The MCTO will remain in effect until the Required Filings are filed or until it is revoked or varied.

The Company confirms that it intends to satisfy the provisions of the alternative information guidelines described in NP 12-203 by issuing bi-weekly default status reports in the form of a news release until it meets the Required Filings requirement. The Company has not taken any steps towards any insolvency proceeding and the Company has no material information relating to its affairs that has not been generally disclosed.

About Altiplano

Altiplano Metals is a growing gold, silver, and copper company focused on the Americas. The Company has a diversified portfolio of assets that include exploration properties, a developing copper/gold/iron mine and a state-of-the-art operating copper/gold and iron processing facility. Altiplano is focused on creating long-term stakeholder value through developing safe and sustainable production, reinvesting into exploration and development, and pursuing acquisition opportunities to provide scalable upside opportunities. Management has a substantial record of success in capitalizing on opportunity, overcoming challenges and building shareholder value.

Altiplano is part of the Metals Group of Companies, led by a dynamic group of resource sector professionals with a long record of success in evaluating and advancing mining projects from exploration through to production, attracting capital, and overcoming adversity to deliver exceptional shareholder value. www.metalsgroup.com.

data.title: Tesla Stock Is Down 8%. The Earnings Call Lacked Meat. — Barrons.com
data.description:
Al Root and Elsa Ohlen

Tesla stock fell Thursday following the company's relatively bland second-quarter earnings report.

Investors wanted to hear more new details about more topics, including Tesla's ambition to become a leader in robo-taxis, or self-driving cars, which has been a key driver for shares of Elon Musk's electric-vehicle maker.

Tesla stock was down 8.5% at $304.27 on Thursday, while the S&P 500 and Dow Jones Industrial Average were up 0.3% and down 0.4%, respectively.

It's always tough to predict Tesla's post-earnings reaction. Shares rose 5.4% after first-quarter numbers that were arguably worse than the second-quarter report. The reaction had Wall Street looking for reasons. A lack of new details is one possible explanation.

The conference call lacked Musk's "usual puffery," wrote GLJ Research analyst Gordon Johnson on Thursday. "On last night's earnings call, TSLA's focus was mostly on stuff it barely sells." He's referring to humanoid robots and self-driving taxi services versus EVs.

It's a bearish take, but Johnson rates the shares Sell and has a Street-low price target of $19.05 a share. The next lowest targets are in the range of $120.

"Robo-taxi [is] the focus but [there is] still some cloudiness," wrote Kallo on Wednesday. "We are mixed on [Tesla's] ability to meet its robo-taxi timelines, cost targets, and scale."

Musk has big plans to scale the robo-taxi business it launched in Austin, Texas in June. He believes Tesla's service can cover half the U.S. population by the end of the year, pending regulatory approvals. That prediction could prove aggressive. Alphabet's Waymo, which completes more than 250,000 self-driving cab rides a week, has ramped up slower, operating in L.A., San Francisco, Phoenix, Austin, and Atlanta. Tesla, however, builds thousands of cars each month, so it has that as a scaling advantage.

Kallo rates Tesla stock at Hold and has a $320 price target. Wedbush analyst Dan Ives rates Tesla stock at Buy and has a $500 price target. Ives felt better about the quarter, calling Tesla's progress "solid" in a Thursday report. He is also encouraged that Musk is spending more time at Tesla these days.

"The autonomous opportunity is worth $1 trillion alone for Tesla," added Ives. "There are still headwinds, tariffs, and clear growth challenges for Tesla over the coming three to six months...but Musk now entering the picture as a wartime CEO to put Tesla on an aggressive [artificial-intelligence]-focused strategy represents the biggest and best possible news for Tesla investors."

One of those growth challenges is a loss of the federal EV tax credit and President Donald Trump's rollback of emission standards. In a call with investors Wednesday afternoon, Musk said that government cuts in support for EV makers could lead to "a few rough quarters." For him, autonomous-driving technology remains the answer to those questions.

"We probably could have a few rough quarters. I'm not saying we will, but we could. Q4, Q1, maybe Q2," Musk said. "But once you get to autonomy at scale in the second half of next year, certainly by the end of next year...I would be surprised if Tesla's economics are not very compelling."

Overall, it was "a mild quarter," wrote Cantor Fitzgerald analyst Andres Sheppard on Thursday. He was encouraged by comments about Tesla's new lower-priced vehicle, which the company said began production in June. He'd still like to see the car, though, noting the "company has not yet unveiled this vehicle."

Tesla needs new models to help boost sales. It sold about 721,000 vehicles in the first half of 2025, down 13% year over year.

Sheppard rates Tesla stock at Buy and has a $355 price target.

Overall, 44% of analysts covering Tesla stock at Buy, according to FactSet. The average Buy-rating ratio for stocks in the S&P 500 is about 55%. The average analyst target price for Tesla stock is about $316.

Write to Elsa Ohlen at elsa.ohlen@barrons.com and Al Root at allen.root@dowjones.com

data.title: NVDA: Nvidia B200 Chips Fuel $1 Billion Black-Market Surge in China
data.description:
July 24 - A Financial Times report reveals that Nvidia's high?end B200 AI processors have quietly made their way into China's data centers, even after new U.S. curbs barred sales of the chip.

Contracts and insider accounts show Chinese distributors offering rack?mounted arrays of eight B200s for roughly RMB 3 million3.5 million ($489,000) apiece, down from more than RMB 4 million in mid?May. The shipments, valued at about $1 billion over three months, bypass official channels under local tariff rules but violate U.S. export restrictions.

Nvidia declined to confirm involvement, noting that stitching together datacenters from smuggled products is a losing proposition without direct company support. Infrastructure kits originated with Super Micro Computer , while partners such as Dell and Asus maintain they enforce strict compliance with export laws.

There are other chips (Nvidia has its H100 and H200 GPUs) sold in online stores and on social media posts in Guangdong and Zhejiang provinces in addition to the B200. According to observers, unregulated exchanges of high-tech AI hardware may compromise the objectives of U.S policy, and ramp up the track of the mushrooming AI industry in China.

In both countries, regulators are said to be considering the gaps in the supply chain as governments attempt to combat the export of chips in violation of restrictions.

data.title: Mergers and acquisitions
data.description:
The following bids, mergers, acquisitions and disposals were reported by 2000 GMT on Thursday:

** Italy's Monte dei Paschi is focused on its takeover offer for Mediobanca , its chief executive said, adding further deals would be something the state-backed bank could consider only in the future.

** Union Pacific , the largest U.S. railroad operator, said it is in advanced talks with rival Norfolk Southern , signaling that a deal to form a $200 billion coast-to-coast rail company could be close - and potentially trigger further consolidation among remaining freight rail giants.

** U.S. Commerce Secretary Howard Lutnick said that TikTok will have to stop operating in the United States if China does not approve a deal for the sale of the Chinese-owned short video app that is used by some 170 million Americans.

** Carrefour , Europe's biggest food retailer, has agreed to sell its loss-making business in Italy to food and drinks manufacturer NewPrinces Group as part of a strategic review kicked off earlier this year.

** European Union judges could review chipmaker Broadcom's $69 billion acquisition of cloud computing firm VMware, after an antitrust complaint opposed the bloc's approval of one of the biggest takeovers in the technology industry.

** EU antitrust regulators will investigate whether U.S. investment firm KKR provided incorrect or misleading information in its 22 billion euro ($26 billion) acquisition of Telecom Italia's (TIM) fixed-line network deal, the European Commission said.

** Danish biotech Bavarian Nordic said it is in discussions with a consortium of Nordic Capital and Permira regarding a potential takeover offer from the two private equity groups, sending its share price up 24%.

** City Office REIT <CIO.N> said it has agreed to be acquired by private investment firm MCME Carell Holdings for $7 per share in cash.

** German electronics retailer Ceconomy confirmed on Thursday it was in advanced negotiations with China's JD.com over a potential public takeover for 4.60 euros per ordinary share.

** France's Pernod Ricard <PERP.PA> has agreed to sell its Imperial Blue whisky brand to India's Tilaknagar Industries <TILK.NS> for an enterprise value of 412.6 million euros ($486 million), as the spirits group boosts its focus on premium labels.

** Healthcare software firm Waystar <WAY.O> said on Wednesday it would buy privately held Iodine Software in a deal worth over $1.25 billion, including debt, to expand its use of artificial intelligence in healthcare payments and reduce claim denials.

** Brazilian food delivery app iFood, owned by Dutch technology investor Prosus <PRX.AS>, is in advanced talks to buy meal voucher company Alelo for about 5 billion reais ($904.75 million), the Valor Economico newspaper reported on Wednesday, citing sources.

** Spain's Sabadell pledged on Thursday to boost profits and payouts for shareholders, including from the recently-agreed sale of UK arm TSB, as the bank set out its case to remain independent in the face of a hostile bid from bigger rival BBVA.

** Dubai-based Sidara is considering reducing the price of its takeover bid for Wood Group , as Britain's financial watchdog has launched a probe into the oilfield services provider, the Financial Times reported on Thursday, citing people familiar with the matter.

** One Rock Capital Partners, a U.S. private equity firm, is one of the few remaining bidders for BP's Castrol lubricants business, Bloomberg News reported on Wednesday, citing people familiar with the matter.

data.title: Treasury Yields Rise as Data, Trade Deals Foster Risk Taking — Market Talk
data.description: U.S. labor data supports a long Fed hold and intensifies a Treasury selloff, pushing yields to a second consecutive rising session. Recent trade deals have been fueling risk-taking, too.Data show layoffs remain contained, removing a potential reason for the Fed to cut interest rates. President Trump, however, keeps calling for fast monetary easing, making markets suspect a more dovish stance lies ahead. June new-home sales grow less than expected, reversing May's decline. Durable goods orders, due tomorrow, are expected to contract 11.1% in a WSJ survey, following May's 16.4% expansion. The 10-year rises 0.021 percentage point to 4.408% and the two-year adds 0.041 p.p. to 3.924%. (paulo.trevisani@wsj.com; @ptrevisani)

data.title: CBOT corn, soybean futures climb on technical trading, trade deal hopes
data.description:
Key points:
  • Corn futures rise on short-covering, consolidation
  • Wheat exports high, but global supply limit gains
  • Soybean futures rise despite sluggish US export sales

Chicago Board of Trade corn futures ticked up on Thursday on signs of short-covering and consolidation, with traders hopeful that President Donald Trump will get foreign buyers to purchase more U.S. farm goods, market analysts said.

While some details of the emerging U.S.-Japanese trade deal are still unclear, Japan's prime minister Shigeru Ishiba said on Thursday that he would do his utmost to ensure the trade deal would be implemented.

Soybean futures ended a choppy day higher on technical trading and trade deal hopes, even as U.S. weekly export sales came in at the lower end of trade estimates and beneficial U.S. crop weather continued to weigh on grain and oilseed markets, analysts said.

Wheat futures recouped some of their losses on signs of export demand, but expectations of ample global supplies and strength in the U.S. dollar capped gains, market analysts said. U.S. wheat is now cheaper than wheat from Europe or top exporter Russia, where a large harvest is beginning to flow into the market.

After the markets closed, scouts on an annual North Dakota crop tour on Thursday projected hard red spring wheat yields in the top-producing state will average 49.0 bushels per acre, down from a record 54.5 bpa last year, according to records going back to 1992. The projection is below the U.S. Department of Agriculture forecast for the state.

On Thursday, the most active CBOT wheat contract Wv1 settled up 1 cent at $5.41-1/2 a bushel. CBOT corn Cv1 ended up 3-1/4 cents at $4.20-3/4 a bushel, while soybeans Sv1 settled 1-1/2 cents higher at $10.24-1/4 a bushel.

Earlier in the session, soybean futures faced spillover pressure from weakness in the CBOT soymeal market, traders said, which turned lower on news that a Chinese buyer has signed a deal this week to import 30,000 metric tons of Argentine meal.

"If China comes to the U.S. last for its soybean needs, eventually that will mean other buyers are going to end up buying U.S. beans and meal," said Jake Hanley, managing director and senior portfolio specialist at Teucrium Trading. "But in the near term? It's not optimistic news."

data.title: DOGE news update: Treasury purchases may kickstart rally to $0.29
data.description:
Key point:

  • Bit Origin’s DOGE purchases for its crypto treasury could limit the downside and open the doors for a rally above $0.29.

China-based pork processing company and Bitcoin miner Bit Origin said on Monday that it had completed the acquisition of 40.5 million Dogecoin for its crypto treasury. Earlier, the firm said on July 17 that it aims to become the largest publicly traded DOGE holder. For that, the firm has entered into agreements with investors to raise $500 million via a combination of share sales and debt offerings.

Could DOGE’s treasury adoption increase demand, boosting prices higher? Let’s analyze the charts to find out.

Dogecoin price prediction

DOGE has been range-bound between $0.14 and $0.29 for several days, indicating buying near the support and selling close to the resistance.

The price turned down from $0.29 on Monday and reached near the 20-day simple moving average ($0.21) on Thursday. Buyers are trying to start a rebound, which could face selling at $0.26 and again at $0.29. If buyers bulldoze their way through, the pair could signal the start of a new up move. The pair may rally toward the pattern target of $0.44.

Instead, if the price turns down and breaks below the 20-day SMA, it signals that the bulls have given up. That may keep the pair inside the range for some more time.

The 20-SMA has started to turn down, and the RSI is in the negative territory, indicating that the bears have the upper hand in the near term. Relief rallies to the 20-SMA are likely to be sold into. If the price turns down from the 20-SMA, the pair risks falling to $0.21.

The first sign of strength will be a close above the 20-SMA. That suggests the selling pressure is reducing. The pair may rally to $0.27 and later to $0.29, where the bears are likely to mount a strong defense. 

This article does not contain investment advice or recommendations. Every investment and trading move involves risk, and readers should conduct their own research when making a decision.

data.title: Corn Futures Higher on Weather Questions — Daily Grain Highlights
data.description:
By Kirk Maltais

  • Corn for December delivery rose 0.8% to $4.21 a bushel on the Chicago Board of Trade on Thursday, finishing on a high note as traders appeared unwilling to commit to the notion that the U.S. corn crop will show little stress from summertime come the harvest season.
  • Soybeans for November delivery rose 0.2% to $10.26 a bushel.
  • Wheat for September delivery rose 0.2% to $5.42 a bushel.

HIGHLIGHTS

Late in the Game: Corn futures turned the corner late in the trading session, finishing the day leading the row crop complex higher.

While the U.S. corn crop is expected to be a record-large, traders appear unwilling to proceed without attaching some amount of weather premium to the futures contract.

"The corn market is acting like it has some support to it," says Charlie Sernatinger of Marex in a note. Reports of some pollination issues for corn in the eastern Corn Belt are giving some traders fuel for their speculation.

"Cosmetically, the plants still look good, but we are going to have to wait another month before we see what the ears look like on the plants," he said.

Corn Confusion: The USDA published a notice following the release of its weekly export sales report that confirmed new large-volume export sales of U.S. corn.

It was supportive for corn futures, but that support dried up after the USDA changed its initial report that 135,000 metric tons were sold to China for delivery during the 2025-26 marketing year. In a correction, the USDA said the actual buyer was South Korea, which traders appeared to find far less exciting.

Retracing Steps: Russian wheat prices were on the rise earlier this week, but that rally appears to have ended, SovEcon said in a note. That is because a lot of newly grown Russian wheat has arrived on Russian markets, the firm said.

"As of July 18, Russia had harvested 23.9 million metric tons of wheat," said SovEcon. "A significant share of the harvested crop is coming from the South (20.9 million metric tons), but harvesting is gradually shifting to the central regions."

The change in direction for Russian prices was felt by other wheat prices around the world, including in Chicago.

INSIGHT

Slashing Its Size: The USDA is moving a significant portion of its headquarters out of Washington, D.C., this after having laid off over 15,000 employees.

In a notice published Thursday signed by Agriculture Secretary Brooke Rollins, the USDA said 15,364 employees have "voluntarily elected deferred resignation," meaning they have submitted their resignation with a delayed date for their exit.

The USDA also said that it was moving D.C. staff to one of five so-called "hubs" that include Raleigh, N.C., Kansas City, Mo., Indianapolis, Fort Collins, Colo., and Salt Lake City.

Over the Water: The cost to ship grains from a port in the U.S. Gulf to Japan reached the highest point since last year, the USDA said in its weekly grain transportation report.

The rate for shipping a metric ton of grain from the Gulf to Japan was $52.75 as of July 17, the highest rate since October and up $4 from the previous week.

President Trump announced a trade deal with Japan this week, which includes many agriculture-centric provisions.

AHEAD

  • The USDA is scheduled to release its twice-a-year cattle inventory report at 3 p.m. EDT Friday.
  • The USDA is due to release its monthly Cattle on Feed Report at 3 p.m. EDT Friday.
  • The USDA is scheduled to release its monthly Cold Storage Report at 3 p.m. EDT Friday.
  • The CFTC is due to release its weekly Commitments of Traders Report at 3:30 p.m. EDT Friday.

Write to Kirk Maltais at kirk.maltais@wsj.com

data.title: Wall Street Giant Bets $250M on Struggling U.S. Farmers in High-Stakes Credit Play
data.description:
Carlyle Group is making a $250 million bet on America's embattled farmersright as bankruptcies across the agriculture sector hit a post-2020 high. Through a forward flow agreement, Carlyle has committed to buying loans originated by FarmOp Capital, a lender focused on helping crop producers bridge the gap between planting and harvest. The working capital loans, typically $3.5 million apiece, are structured around staple grains like corn, soybeans, and wheat. But instead of relying on land as collaterala nonstarter for many rentersthese loans are backed by crops, crop insurance, and forward contracts, giving farmers the financial flexibility traditional banks no longer offer.

It's a timely move. Small-business bankruptcies among farmers and fishermen have surged this year as falling commodity prices, rising input costs, and weaker Chinese demand squeeze margins. The industry is going through a tough period, FarmOp CEO Keir Renick said. Farmers can get into trouble when prices go down and they are not hedged. Most banks have pulled back from lending to landless operators, creating a vacuum that alternative lenders like FarmOp are stepping in to fill. These loans, often lasting about 18 months, are tailored to experienced operators who rent land and need short-term capital to stay afloat.

For Carlyle, this isn't a one-off. The firm is steadily building its asset-backed credit platformnow managing $9 billionthrough exposure to real-world assets like residential solar loans and fintech lending deals. Akhil Bansal, who leads Carlyle's asset-backed finance division, described farm lending as a very stable but growing asset class, thanks to its multi-layered contractual protections. The firm's approach signals a broader strategy: back specialty lenders that solve real economic problems, especially in sectors where traditional capital has dried up.

data.title: UNION PACIFIC CORP SEC 10-Q Report
data.description:
Union Pacific Corp, a leading railroad operator in North America, has released its Form 10-Q report for the second quarter of 2025. The report highlights the company's robust financial performance and significant operational improvements, driven by volume increases, core pricing gains, and enhanced efficiency. Below is a detailed summary of the key financial and business highlights from the report.

Financial Highlights

Total Operating Revenues: $6,154 million, increased by 2% compared to the same period in 2024 driven by volume increases and core pricing gains.

Operating Income: $2,525 million, increased by 5% reflecting top-line growth, solid operational performance, and productivity gains.

Net Income: $1,876 million, increased by 12% due to improved operational performance and a reduction in income tax expense.

Earnings Per Share - Diluted: $3.15, increased from $2.74 in the same period of 2024, reflecting higher net income and share repurchases.

Operating Ratio: 59.0%, improved by 1.0 point from the previous year, driven by core pricing gains, productivity initiatives, and volume growth.

Business Highlights

Revenue Segments: Freight revenues increased by 4% in the second quarter of 2025 compared to the same period in 2024, driven by volume increases and core pricing gains. Bulk shipments, including grain and grain products, fertilizer, food and refrigerated, and coal and renewables, saw a 10% increase in freight revenues due to volume growth and core pricing gains.

Geographical Performance: Freight revenues from shipments to and from Mexico increased by 1% to $751 million in the second quarter of 2025, driven by volume growth in grain, petroleum products, and intermodal shipments, despite a decline in automotive-related shipments.

Sales Units: Revenue carloads increased by 4% in the second quarter of 2025 compared to 2024, with significant growth in coal and renewables (30% increase) and grain and grain products (8% increase).

Operational Efficiency: Freight car velocity improved by 10% in the second quarter of 2025 compared to 2024, reflecting enhanced operational performance. Terminal dwell time decreased by 7%, and locomotive productivity increased by 5%, indicating better asset utilization.

Future Outlook: The company plans to invest approximately $3.4 billion in capital expenditures in 2025, focusing on growth strategy, safety, and operational efficiency improvements. This includes modernization of the locomotive fleet and acquiring freight cars to support replacement and growth opportunities.

Service Performance: The intermodal service performance index improved by 6 points, and the manifest service performance index improved by 13 points in the second quarter of 2025 compared to 2024, demonstrating enhanced service reliability.

Workforce Productivity: Workforce productivity increased by 9% in the second quarter of 2025 compared to 2024, as the company handled more volume with fewer employees, reflecting improved efficiency.

Train Length: Average train length increased by 2% in the second quarter of 2025 compared to 2024, driven by initiatives to improve train length, particularly in coal and grain train volumes.

SEC Filing: UNION PACIFIC CORP [ UNP ] - 10-Q - Jul. 24, 2025

data.title: RLI CORP SEC 10-Q Report
data.description:
RLI Corp, a specialty insurance company, has released its latest Form 10-Q report, showcasing robust financial and operational performance for the quarter ended June 30, 2025. The report highlights significant growth in revenue and net earnings, driven by higher net premiums earned and net investment income. Additionally, the company continues to demonstrate strong operational efficiency and a strategic focus on niche markets.

Financial Highlights

  • Consolidated Revenue: $499.8 million, reflecting a significant increase from $416.4 million in the same period last year, driven by higher net premiums earned and net investment income.
  • Net Earnings: $124.3 million, up from $82.0 million in the prior year period, indicating strong profitability.
  • Basic Net Earnings Per Share: $1.35, compared to $0.90 in the previous year, showing improved earnings per share performance.
  • Diluted Net Earnings Per Share: $1.34, up from $0.89 in the prior year, reflecting enhanced shareholder value.
  • Earnings Before Income Taxes: $156.5 million, a substantial increase from $103.3 million in the previous year, highlighting improved operational efficiency and investment performance.

Business Highlights

  • Revenue Segments: The company operates through three main segments: Casualty, Property, and Surety. The Casualty segment includes commercial excess, personal umbrella, general liability, transportation, and management liability coverages. The Property segment primarily consists of commercial fire, hurricane, earthquake, and marine coverages. The Surety segment offers contract surety coverages, including payment and performance bonds, as well as commercial and transactional bonds.
  • Casualty Segment Performance: The Casualty segment reported net premiums earned of $234.6 million for the three months ended June 30, 2025, showing growth from $209.1 million in the same period of 2024. This segment includes products like commercial excess and personal umbrella, which saw significant growth.
  • Property Segment Performance: The Property segment reported net premiums earned of $130.7 million for the three months ended June 30, 2025, compared to $134.1 million in the same period of 2024. The segment includes commercial property and marine coverages.
  • Surety Segment Performance: The Surety segment reported net premiums earned of $36.6 million for the three months ended June 30, 2025, slightly up from $35.9 million in the same period of 2024. This segment includes transactional, commercial, and contract surety bonds.
  • Operational Strategy: The company focuses on niche markets and developing unique products tailored to customer needs, maintaining a diverse product portfolio and underwriting for profit in all market conditions.
  • Underwriting Profitability: The company achieved its 29th consecutive year of underwriting profitability in 2024, with an average combined ratio of 88.1 over this period, emphasizing its focus on sound risk selection and discipline.
  • Future Outlook: The company plans to continue focusing on underwriting profitability with a secondary focus on premium growth where underwriting profit exists, rather than general premium growth or market share.

SEC Filing: RLI CORP [ RLI ] - 10-Q - Jul. 24, 2025
